skill.name: ubiquitous-language
skill.description: Extract a domain-driven design ubiquitous language glossary from the current conversation, flag ambiguities, choose canonical terms, and save the result to UBIQUITOUS_LANGUAGE.md.
skill.summary: Create a clear DDD glossary from conversation context and keep domain terms consistent.
skill.author: mattpocock

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged external commands, weak cryptography, and system reconnaissance, but review found these are Markdown inline-code, fenced examples, or table text in SKILL.md. The only confirmed risk is expected filesystem access to read and write UBIQUITOUS_LANGUAGE.md in the working directory, so the skill is low risk and safe to publish.

low_findings.title: Expected Local Glossary File Access
low_findings.description: The skill instructs the agent to write UBIQUITOUS_LANGUAGE.md and read it during later runs. This is a legitimate documentation workflow, but users should expect one local file to be created or updated in the working directory.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file access instructions are explicit and limited to UBIQUITOUS_LANGUAGE.md. There is no evidence of broad filesystem traversal or hidden file access.

low_findings.title: External Command Findings Are Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: The scanner interpreted Markdown inline code and a fenced Markdown example as Ruby or shell backtick execution. The reviewed lines describe output formatting and terminology examples; they do not instruct the agent to run shell commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The evidence is plain Markdown syntax and quoted dialogue. No executable script, command runner, or user-controlled command construction appears in the file.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Finding Is Frontmatter Text
low_findings.description: The scanner flagged SKILL.md line 3 as weak cryptography, but the line is a natural language description of the skill. No cryptographic algorithm or implementation appears there.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 3 contains only frontmatter description text about a DDD glossary. I found no evidence of cryptographic code or algorithm selection.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Findings Are Table Text
low_findings.description: The scanner flagged SKILL.md lines 31 and 38 as system reconnaissance, but these lines are Markdown table rows in an output example. They do not collect host, process, network, or account information.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines are table formatting for glossary examples. I found no evidence of reconnaissance commands or requests for system metadata.

content.value_statement: Teams often use the same domain terms in different ways, which causes unclear requirements and inconsistent software design. This skill extracts terms from the conversation, chooses canonical language, flags ambiguity, and saves a DDD glossary.
actual_capabilities: Scans the current conversation for domain nouns, verbs, and concepts.
Identifies ambiguous terms, overloaded words, and duplicate names for the same concept.
Proposes canonical terms and lists aliases that the team should avoid.
Groups related terms into glossary sections when clear clusters exist.
Writes the glossary to UBIQUITOUS_LANGUAGE.md in the working directory.
Updates an existing glossary when invoked again in the same conversation.
limitations: It only uses conversation context and any existing UBIQUITOUS_LANGUAGE.md file.
It does not verify terms against external product documentation or source systems.
It cannot resolve domain disputes without enough input from subject matter experts.
It may overwrite the glossary file when updating the documented language.

use_cases.title: Align Product and Engineering Terms
use_cases.description: Turn a planning discussion into a shared glossary that reduces ambiguity before implementation starts.

use_cases.title: Document a Domain Model
use_cases.description: Extract core entities, relationships, and forbidden aliases from a technical conversation about a bounded context.

use_cases.title: Refine Team Vocabulary Over Time
use_cases.description: Update an existing glossary as new concepts appear and previous definitions become more precise.

prompt_templates.title: Create a First Glossary
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner prompt after a short domain discussion.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a ubiquitous language glossary from this conversation and save it to UBIQUITOUS_LANGUAGE.md.

prompt_templates.title: Clarify Ambiguous Terms
prompt_templates.scenario: Use when several terms may mean the same thing.
prompt_templates.prompt: Extract the domain terms from this conversation, choose canonical names, and flag any ambiguous or overloaded terms.

prompt_templates.title: Organize by Subdomain
prompt_templates.scenario: Use when the discussion covers several business areas.
prompt_templates.prompt: Build a DDD glossary grouped by natural subdomains. Include definitions, aliases to avoid, relationships, and flagged ambiguity.

prompt_templates.title: Refresh an Existing Glossary
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced prompt after the team has continued the discussion.
prompt_templates.prompt: Read the existing UBIQUITOUS_LANGUAGE.md, incorporate the new discussion, revise definitions, and rewrite the example dialogue.

output_examples.input: A checkout discussion mentions customer, user, account, order, invoice, fulfillment, and shipment.
output: A glossary with separate Customer and User definitions.
A relationships section that explains when invoices are created.
A flagged ambiguity note for account when it refers to two different concepts.

output_examples.input: A platform team discusses sync service, filesystem layer, Docker layer, sandbox, bundle, and copy operations.
output: A grouped glossary for service, layer, and artifact terms.
Aliases to avoid for terms that sound similar but describe different responsibilities.
A short dialogue that shows precise use of the selected terminology.

best_practices: Give the skill enough conversation history from domain experts and implementers.
Review canonical terms with stakeholders before using them in tickets or code.
Re-run the skill after major product decisions so the glossary stays current.
anti_patterns: Using it as a replacement for direct domain expert validation.
Including generic programming terms that have no domain meaning.
Keeping conflicting aliases without choosing a preferred canonical term.

faq.question: What file does this skill create?
faq.answer: It creates or updates UBIQUITOUS_LANGUAGE.md in the working directory.

faq.question: Does it use external services?
faq.answer: No evidence found in SKILL.md indicates network access or external service calls.

faq.question: Can it update an existing glossary?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill instructs the agent to read the existing glossary and incorporate new discussion.

faq.question: What makes it useful for DDD work?
faq.answer: It focuses on canonical terms, aliases to avoid, relationships, and ambiguity in domain language.

faq.question: Can it decide business meaning by itself?
faq.answer: No. It can propose definitions, but domain experts should confirm important terms.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report marks it as compatible with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
